source: Low hematocrit (percentage of red blood cells in the blood) is often observed in psoriasis patients, reflecting anemia.
Anemia (low hemoglobin) is common in psoriasis, particularly in severe cases, due to chronic inflammation and potential iron deficiency.
Low mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) can be seen in psoriasis, potentially indicating hypochromic anemia or iron deficiency.
Low mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) can be seen in psoriasis, potentially indicating hypochromic anemia or iron deficiency.
Low mean corpuscular volume (MCV) can be seen in psoriasis, potentially indicating microcytic anemia or iron deficiency.
Elevated platelet count (thrombocytosis) is a common finding in psoriasis, possibly related to inflammation and increased platelet production.
Low red blood cell count (RBC) can be seen in psoriasis, particularly in severe cases, due to anemia.
Elevated RDW can indicate increased red blood cell production variability, which may be seen in psoriasis due to inflammation.
Elevated white blood cell count (leukocytosis) can be seen in psoriasis due to inflammation and immune activation.
